[ A . G . P . L ]
Theory Simulator

PUMPING LEMMA
MEETS
ANTIGRAVITY

A conceptual bridge between computational theory and fictional physics.

"This project uses the pumping lemma as inspiration only. It does not represent real physical laws."

SCROLL TO EXPLORE
01

What is the Pumping Lemma?

In the Theory of Computation, the Pumping Lemma is a mathematical tool used to prove that a specific language is NOT regular.


It states that any sufficiently long string in a regular language must contain a section that can be repeated (or "pumped") any number of times, and the resulting string will still belong to the same language.

02

The Core Formula

We divide a long string s into three parts: s = xyz.

  • x: The beginning of the string.
  • y: The middle part (the loop).
  • z: The end of the string.

According to the lemma, we can repeat the y part zero or multiple times (xyⁿz), and the new string remains valid.

03

The Intuition

Why does this repetition happen? Regular languages are processed by Finite State Machines (FSMs). Since states are finite, a long enough string will inevitably visit the same state twice, creating a loop.


For example, the language aⁿbⁿ (equal number of a's and b's) cannot be regular because an FSM cannot "count" infinitely without running out of memory (states) to remember how many a's to match with b's.

04

The Antigravity Simulation

How does this connect to our fictional simulation?

We treat the "y" component as a repeatable energy cycle inside an antigravity engine.

  • x = Engine Startup Phase
  • y = Anti-Graviton Pumping Cycle
  • z = Stabilization Phase

Each time we "pump" y (evaluate xyⁿz), the system's effect amplifies—conceptually reducing gravitational pull more and more with each loop.

Ready to bend gravity?

Experience the pumping cycle in real-time.
